summary.en: The historic and commercial core of Bisbee, this is a dense, vibrant district built into Tombstone Canyon, defined by its hundreds of public staircases and bohemian, Old West vibe.
A living art installation and mid-century time capsule, this area's heart is Erie Street, a block meticulously preserved to look as it did in the 1950s, creating a surreal and photogenic experience.
One of Arizona's first planned communities, this quieter, residential district contrasts with Old Bisbee's verticality, featuring graceful homes, wide streets, and a deep sporting and labor history.

description.en: Located in Arizona's longest continuously operated hotel, this historic saloon is a living piece of Bisbee history, perfect for soaking in a turn-of-the-century atmosphere.
The town's defining feature, these intricate networks of public concrete stairs connect homes and offer unique architectural views, serving as the true thoroughfares of Old Bisbee.
A viewpoint offering a staggering perspective of a massive, inactive open-pit copper mine, whose sheer scale and colorful geology are a beautiful reminder of the industry that built Bisbee.
A major town landmark and reputedly haunted historic hotel. Its lobby and facade are a testament to the town's prosperous mining past.

The main attraction of Lowell, this street is curated to be a perfect mid-century American snapshot, with vintage cars and period-correct storefronts that make you feel like you've stepped onto a movie set.
A unique hotel experience featuring a collection of perfectly restored vintage travel trailers from the 1940s and '50s, complementing the time-capsule vibe of the area.

One of America's oldest professional baseball stadiums, this site holds a complex past as both a place of recreation and the location where striking miners were held during the 1917 Bisbee Deportation.
Home to a quirky historical error, this spot features a marker incorrectly claiming it as the Continental Divide, making it a perfect quest for lovers of odd local lore.
The streets of Warren feature beautiful examples of Arts and Crafts and Bungalow-style homes, telling a story of the town's class structure through architecture.

prompt.en: Find a long, steep public staircase and take a triumphant selfie from the top or bottom.
Capture a photo that showcases at least five different colors on the historic buildings of Tombstone Canyon.
Many walls and alleys hide small murals or art pieces. Find a piece of street art that isn't on a main road and photograph it.
Take a photo of a historic building that looks like it could be haunted. Use a black and white filter for extra spookiness.
Frame a shot that shows how homes are built directly into the steep canyon walls, one on top of the other.
Find a local cafe or diner and photograph your meal. Imagine what a copper miner might have eaten a century ago.
Find a storefront or home with quirky, artistic decorations and capture a close-up of your favorite detail.
Photograph a piece of architecture that feels like it's straight out of an old Western movie.
Find a historic-looking bar and take a photo of your drink on the counter, capturing the atmosphere.
Climb any set of stairs and capture a panoramic view of the town nestled in the canyon.
Find one of Old Bisbee's narrowest streets and take a photo looking down its length, emphasizing the tight space.
Capture the reflection of a historic building in a modern shop window, blending old and new.
Pose with a vintage car on Erie Street as if you've just stepped out of it in 1958.
Take a photo of a storefront that looks completely untouched by the modern world.
Find a classic car and take a close-up, artistic shot of a detail like a hood ornament, tail fin, or chrome bumper.
Capture the quiet, surreal emptiness of Erie Street in a single photograph.
Find an old sign or advertisement on a building and photograph it. What was it selling?
Photograph one of the vintage trailers at The Shady Dell, focusing on its unique retro design.
Use a sepia or faded color filter to take a photo that looks like a genuine old photograph from the 1950s.
Frame a shot that includes a classic car, a storefront, and the American flag to capture a sense of mid-century Americana.
Take a photo of a friend (or a selfie) posing as if they are an actor on a movie set for a film set in the 50s.
Find a modern object (like a smartphone) and photograph it against the vintage backdrop of Erie Street to create a striking contrast.
Photograph a classic neon or painted sign on one of the buildings.
Take a picture of the old-fashioned gas pumps, imagining a time when gas was 25 cents a gallon.
Find a classic Arts and Crafts or Bungalow-style home and take a photo of its inviting front porch.
Capture the symmetry and wider, greener streets of Warren, contrasting it with the chaotic layout of Old Bisbee.
Take a selfie in front of the historic Warren Ballpark, home to over a century of baseball.
Find a historical marker or plaque in the neighborhood and take a photo of it. What story does it tell?
Photograph a quiet residential street, capturing the peaceful atmosphere of this historic suburb.
Find two houses side-by-side built in different styles and capture them in one photo to show the area's architectural diversity.
Warren was designed around a central park. Take a photo that captures the feeling of this community green space.
Find a particularly grand or well-preserved historic home and photograph its most impressive feature.
Take a photo at the Warren Ballpark that feels somber, reflecting its role in the 1917 Bisbee Deportation.
Take a photo from the perspective of someone sitting on a porch, looking out at the street.
Capture an image that represents sports or recreation in the neighborhood, whether it's the ballpark, a basketball hoop, or a local park.
If you find the erroneous Continental Divide marker at Mule Pass, take a photo celebrating this quirky piece of local lore.
